scenario: Lattice Insurance, a regional insurer, processes 4500 claim intake requests per week through Camunda 8.8 user tasks rendered in Tasklist. The claim intake form has a text field whose Key is set to claimant.email, the same value already held in a process variable, and the team needs that single Key to drive both initial loading and submission within 3 seconds of the task opening.
question: What dual role does the Key attribute play during this user task lifecycle?

options.text: The Key only loads the initial value from the process variable into the field and is entirely skipped on task submission
The Key only maps the submitted value back to a process variable and does not influence the initial value rendered in the field
The Key maps a process variable into the field as the initial value and writes the field value back to that variable on submission
The Key identifies the field for Tasklist styling rules and has no role in moving data into or out of the process variables

a.text: Incorrect. The docs describe the Key as serving a dual purpose, so it also participates in mapping the field data back to the matching process variable on submission, not only on initial load.

b.text: Incorrect. The Key correlates with a process variable on both ends of the task: it serves as the initial value of the field and is updated on submission, not just one direction.

c.text: Correct. The Key serves a dual purpose: it maps process data to the field during the initial loading of the form and maps the field data back to the process variable during form submission.

d.text: Incorrect. The Key is a data-binding identifier between the field and a process variable; it has no role in Tasklist styling or in any presentation concern.

explanation: The Key attribute on a form field has two duties: it pulls the matching process variable into the field as the initial value when the task opens in Tasklist, and it writes the submitted field value back into that same process variable on form submission.
